headlines.title: NEARLY $4,000 IS RAISED BY TEAMS IN Y. W. C. A. WORK
headlines.fullText: Eight teams working for the $25.000 the Young Women's Christian Association is undertaking to raise , this week reported at the luncheon monday that they had raised $3,763.60 in which Mrs. J. H. . Smith is captain raised the most, totaling $2,727 in tem Mrs. C. T. Prewett's team raised 1410 Short's team raised $286 in three subscriptions. The citizens' team raised attptions citizens od Huffman's team raised $40 in three mbacriptions. the business women's team raised $26.60 in six subscriptions and the young men's team raised $25 In one subscription More teams will be at work later in the week. I believe in the Yours Was Christian Association," E. E. Baldridge. Fand Monday. can tantes Bank, said Monday, because I: ministers through its various departments to the whole woman. It is perhaps today one of the greatest moral factors in our civie life.

headlines.title: MARSHALL ELK GIVEN PARDON BY GOVERNOR
headlines.subtitle: George H. Barnes Released and Portion of Fine Remitted in Pro Test Case.
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. AUSTIN, May 25 .- A pardon was issued today by the governor to George H. Barnes, former exalted ruled of the was fined $6,000 tor operating a buffet In dry territory and fer operating cold storage plant and who also was mond ane confinement in the county jail. The court of criminal appeals had affirmed the case and last Friday the mandate was issued. Ao thereafter friends for Darnes appealed to the governor a pardon and this was granted day. The governor remitted $5,700 of the fine and also the jail sentence, and the balance of the fine, $250, the governor indicated, should be paid by Barnes for the violation of the law.

headlines.title: YOUTH REPORTED DROWNED, LIVING; TWO IN CUSTODY
headlines.fullText: MARLIN, Texas, May 26,-Erter Gillespie of Mart, Texas, who was reported drowned in the Brazos Ing Thursday, along with Parker Townsend of Mart, was brought here by police officers today alive and well sat placed in custody pending investigation of the river accident. Monta Miles, who was with Giltesgin and Townsend, Is in jal! here charpe with thett of a boat, having borrowed it, and had written a statement to the justice of the peace, describing the drowning accident. Before making thu statement Miles had reported that both Gillespie and Townsend wer drowned, The statement to the justice, however, declared this story untrue is set. eral important features and decities for Gillespie then begin ers Gillespie corroborated the statemes! had Grownel The that Townsend fag Grupper The bo lice are now searching for Townseeds bedy.

headlines.title: GANG HOMICIDES IN N. Y. AROUSE POLICE
headlines.subtitle: Seven Cases in Thirty-Six Hours Starts Investigation-All Former Records Broken.
headlines.fullText: NEW YORK, May 25-The police and the district attorney's office today are investigating the seven homicide cases reported within the thirty-six hours up to midnight last night. Four of the seven victims are dead and the other three are not expected to live The dead lost their lives in this is the largest number of hom !. cide cases in the same length of time in many years. The latest victim. Fred Hillock, staggered into the New York hospital th. The pollce questioned sixty members of # social club housed in the old Gen. Franz Seigel mansion, in Aninedon Square, where they believed Hilleck was shot. He is expected to die.

headlines.title: SUES FOR $10,000
headlines.subtitle: R. L. Sherley Claims Damages for Sen's Death.
headlines.fullText: B. L. Sherley filed suit for $10,000 damages in the district court Monday against the Cleveland Construction Company and the Fort Worth Power and Light Company as the result of the death of his son. Albert Sherley, Toung Sherley was employed as a theredone were was then being done here by the con- wer company and was instantly lil'led by coming in contact with a live wire.

headlines.title: KONE GOES TO CAPITAL TO DISCUSS MARKETING
headlines.subtitle: Texas Commissioner to Meet With Ranchmen and Others at Conference.
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, May BimActing on an in- Houston, Commissioner of Agriculture Kone will go to Washington in attesa to attend : Conterence or serem comme: pioners and ranchmen, which is to meet eine marketing agricultura products and also of sheep and wool. Commissioner Kone left today Denton, where he attends the closing exercises at the College of Industrial Arts, then he goes to Dallas to deliver an address at the State Ginners' Association He then proceeds to Washington for the conference.

headlines.title: Car Jumps Track, Three Hurt.
headlines.fullText: QUANAH, Texas. May 28,-An elect trie mertinn car on the at Loud track here this morning. Injuring three men. Robert Fitzgerald, foreman, had Broken both legs broken and his recovery is doubtful

headlines.title: ST. ANDREW'S MEN TO ENTERTAIN WOMEN
headlines.fullText: Every member of the Men's Club of St. Andrew's parish is expected to bring a lady with him to the meetingz which will be held in the parish house at s. p. m. Tuesday, One usb usual features of the meeting wub that the women mil nord do anything except enjoy themselves. The men will prepare the refreshments don the work. There will be a number of musicul numbers. The St. Andrew's Choristers, a double quartet, will sing. There will Be a lecture with lantern slides on the Panama canal work. Refreshments will be served.

headlines.title: WOMAN HAD PART IN FORGERY CASE, CLAIM DETECTIVES
headlines.fullText: A woman had a part in the wholesale forgery of Katy pay checks, the castribution cien temcom by the Fort Worth Samme according to detectives. They say the body of the checks and perhaps actual forgeries was the work of a woman. As te where this woman is located, or as to the place where the checks were made out. detectives are still in the dark. Meanwhile, Katy agents are making a personal investigation in an effort to trail the forgerien to headquarters. They have notified the banks and merchants in the cities and towns all along the line to beware of the fora. trist They' believe of the pan ot men who worked Fort Worth and San Antonio are only part of an erganined a bundle of bad checks at the same time. He refuses to say who he was or where he came from. The prisoner is holding the detectives at bay on the deadand the wholesale forgeries. Confronted with detectives and a special agent of the Katy, the prisoner only smiled and "kidded" them. Detectives say he has not jet a word allp him that would be or any service in their investigation. He talks plentifully, and if he is worried he does not show It. Checks to the total of $500 were passed off on San Antonio merchants Thursday. The man, who admits cashing five of the forged checks on Fort Worth merchants saturday, has been identified by Detective Chief John Connelley an Jeff Charum, a noted forger and Bix times a convict, who escaped from the penitentiary ruquer un nume ett 1918. This Identification in supported by a photograph of Sharum sent out Formal charges of forgery will be filed against the man by merece complaints will specify the forgery and passing of checks at Burton's, Washer's. Meacham's, Monnig's and Jamieson-Diggs Co. The pay checks which were cashed by A. & L. August. H. Brann & Co. and Stonestreet & Davis are attributed to the alleged confederate-the man who took fright and retreated when one of his had checks was stopped at Stripling a. The man now in jail admits that there was a confederate at work with

headlines.title: TO NAME DIRECTORS OF DALLAS RESERVE BANK
headlines.subtitle: Bankers of Oklahoma to Meet Friday for Classes A and B Selections.
headlines.fullText: DALLAS, May 25- Bankers of that part of Oklahoma fiets bed in the Dal: meet here next Friday to select class A and class B directors of the Dallas regional reserve banke. Hankers of the entire district will meet here Saturday to pick their nomi- but may la Fen wer Fog ge the reserve bank. W. B. Harrison, secretary of the Oklahoma Bankers' Association, called the meeting of Oklahoma bankers today.

headlines.title: WOULD SAVE BODIES AS WELL AS SOULS
headlines.fullText: KANSAS CITY, May 26-For some years the churches have labored with had bodies. Now coincident with the movement in all public institutions to get into a more intimate relation with the lives of the people they deal with. the churches have rediscovered human nature. This was the statement made to the general assembly of the Presbyterian. Southern. in session here today, by Rev. J. 8. Lyons of Louisville, Ky- a preface to the introduction of the report of the joint Presbyterian bod- les in the United States, appointed to tian faith and Christian servies. Camauan service. take a more aggressive part in the social and industrial life of the community.

headlines.title: TELEGRAPHERS FORM ORGANIZATION HERE
headlines.subtitle: Chairman of Rock Island Order, With Headquarters in Topeka, Makes
headlines.fullText: The organization of the "F. O. R. T- the Fort Worth Order of Railway Telegraphers, was completed meeting at the Chamber of Commerce Sunday night. W. T. Brown, chall. Telegraphera, Railroad Telegraphers, with headquarters at Topeka, Kan, made the principal address. The officers of the Fort Worth order are Sam Heene, president: W. H. Terhune, first vice president. W. H. Robinson, second vice president.

headlines.title: LAKE WORTH CLAIMS CONTINUE TO COME
headlines.fullText: Firat claims filed against the city sex for damages only for the overflow Hausvater con Monday ale damage der er overrien and one of last Saturday as well. The Bew claims enteres Monday are by C Lithleum and Allen Birdwell for $1.300; by G. A. Rice for 1750 and bro d Rice for $450. With the former claims filed the total damage claimed so far Is $8,156. Fresh overflow of lands above the Lake Worth reservoir May 28 occurred. according to claims filed Monday with the city commission. The claimants assert that land held by them above Lake Worth have been put under water during the floods as a result of the dam construction at lake. When the dam is finished the water will stand approximately twenty feet higher than at present. The bridge connecting the north and south sides of the embankment is un. water. Though the water is now tante ing. it is likely work will be delayed for some time

headlines.title: ENNIS ORDINANCE IS VALID, RULES COURT.
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON. May 25 .- The supreme court today dismissed an appeal from the supreme court of Texas which held valld an ordinance of file city of Ennis. Texas, anEuning the franchise of the Ennis Waterworks Company.

headlines.title: COOKE COUNTY WHEAT IN FINE CONDITION
headlines.subtitle: Farmers' Union Official Says Rain Was Not in Sufficient Quan- ty to Hurt.
headlines.fullText: "Wheat conditions in Cool. county are ideal weeurding to A. H. MicDaniel of Munster, chairman of the executive committee of the Farmers' Union, who was here Monday. The harvest will be about ten days late as a result of the cool weather." wald MeDaniel, but that will be an advantage to the wheat. We did not have nearly so much rain as they did In many sections, and there was prac- Hoy no due to the wheat from that cause. It sure is looking fine now and the prospect for a big yield could not be better.

headlines.title: Del Rio Gets Rain
headlines.fullText: DEL RIO, Texas, May 25-One and wenn swelled the amount for the past week here to over seven inches.

headlines.title: ACCUSED OF KILLING BRIDE OF THREE MONTHS.
headlines.fullText: GREENVILLE, Texas, May 25 .- George Mayfield was arrested here Sunday, charged with killing his bride of three months at Brenham. three weeks ago. He is being held for Brenham officers.
